title: Tablet weaving a basic four-card band
subtitle: Using four square cards to weave a narrow patterned band in two colours
excerpt: Tablet weaving uses small square cards with holes at each corner as the shaft system. Threading two colours through the holes and rotating the cards produces a two-colour diagonal pattern in a narrow band. A four-card tablet-woven band is the standard starting point: narrow, manageable, and producing clear pattern structure that scales up to more cards without changing the fundamental technique.
sourceNotes: Tablet weaving is an ancient textile technique found across Europe and Central Asia from the Bronze Age onward. The four-card starting pattern is a standard contemporary teaching method. Technique is observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A small square card with holes at each corner used in tablet weaving. Each hole carries one warp thread. Rotating the card lifts different threads through the warp, creating pattern.

The turning of the tablet-weaving cards to create the pattern shed. Rotating forward (away from the weaver) and backward (toward the weaver) produces different thread arrangements and colour patterns.

content.text: each threaded with two colours (one through holes A and B, one through holes C and D) produce a diagonal-stripe band when the cards are rotated continuously forward. A four-forward, four-backward
produces a chevron. Four cards and 16 thread ends is small enough to tension between a doorknob and a belt loop tied around the waist.

items.name: Square tablet-weaving cards, 6 cm, with holes at each corner
Warp thread in two colours, smooth and strong (cotton or linen)
items.qty: 2 x 2 m lengths per colour per card (32 m total for 4 cards, 2 colours)

items.name: Belt clip or door handle (for back tension)
Weft thread (can be either warp colour or a third colour)

content.text: Mark the holes A, B, C, D on each card. Thread the first colour through holes A and B on all four cards. Thread the second colour through holes C and D on all four cards. All four cards should be threaded in the same direction (S or Z threading: the direction of the thread as it enters the hole). Align all cards in a stack.

Tie all 16 thread ends together at one end and attach to a fixed point (a doorknob or a loom back beam). Hold the cards at the working end and lean back to create tension. The band grows between the fixed point and the cards.
Step 3: Rotate and pass the weft

Rotate all four cards simultaneously a quarter turn away from you (forward rotation). A shed opens between the two groups of threads. Pass the weft through the shed. Beat down with a finger or a flat stick. Rotate another quarter turn forward. Pass the weft through the new shed. Beat. After four forward rotations, the pattern will be visible in two-colour diagonal stripes.

After 4 forward rotations, switch to 4 backward rotations (toward you). The diagonal stripes will reverse direction. Continuing to alternate 4 forward / 4 backward produces a chevron or zigzag pattern.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two common problems for first-time tablet weavers.

items.symptom: Weft visible on the surface instead of only warp threads showing
items.cause: Weft is too thick relative to the warp, or the beat is too light.
items.fix: Use a weft thread the same weight or finer than the warp. Beat more firmly after each pick.

items.symptom: Cards twist together and the warp tangles
items.cause: Rotating all cards forward without reversing causes the warp to twist progressively.
items.fix: Every 4 to 8 rotations, work a reversal sequence (4 backward) to unwind the accumulated twist. Or use a free-hanging back attachment that allows the warp to rotate.
